verification.note: MF capital-gains country map checked against ITAT Mumbai Anushaka Sanjay Shah (ITA No. 174/MUM/2025, 26 Mar 2025) and current NRI filing guides. Treaty article numbers are indicative — confirm the exact article in your country's DTAA before filing.

itat_mf_ruling.case: Anushaka Sanjay Shah v. ITO (ITAT Mumbai)
itat_mf_ruling.holding: MF units are not company shares; gains fall under the DTAA residual clause and may be taxable only in the country of residence when that clause grants exclusive taxing rights there.

Singapore.note: ITAT Mumbai (Mar 2025) held Indian MF gains taxable only in Singapore. Singapore does not levy personal capital-gains tax, so effective Indian tax can be zero — but you must still file an ITR in India to claim the exemption and recover any TDS.

United Arab Emirates.note: Residual clause: gains taxable only in the UAE. No personal income tax there.

Saudi Arabia.note: Residual clause: gains taxable only in Saudi Arabia.

Kuwait.note: Residual clause: gains taxable only in Kuwait.

Oman.note: Residual clause: gains taxable only in Oman.

Qatar.note: Residual clause: gains taxable only in Qatar.

Germany.note: Treaty assigns MF gains to Germany; taxed per German law, not Indian rates.

Italy.note: Residual clause: gains taxable only in Italy.

Malaysia.note: Residual clause: gains taxable only in Malaysia.

Nepal.note: Residual clause: gains taxable only in Nepal.

Philippines.note: Residual clause: gains taxable only in the Philippines.

United States.note: India-US DTAA taxes these gains in India at Indian rates. You may claim foreign tax credit in the US via Form 67.

United Kingdom.note: Taxable in India; UK residents can claim foreign tax credit at home.

Canada.note: Both countries may tax; pay in India first, then claim foreign tax credit in Canada.

Australia.note: Taxable in India at Indian rates.

Hong Kong.note: Taxable in India despite Hong Kong's territorial tax system.
